prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 42-year-old man.

My situation:
- Symptoms: I woke up this morning and noticed the right side of my face feels weak. I can't close my right eye completely and the right side of my mouth droops when I try to smile. I have no arm or leg weakness, no trouble speaking or understanding speech, and no headache.
- History: I had a mild cold about a week ago. No diabetes. No history of stroke or heart disease.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): This pattern could be Bell’s palsy, which is a sudden weakness of one side of the face and often happens after a recent viral illness. The fact that your eye and mouth are both affected fits that. However, any sudden new facial droop can also be a stroke, even if you do not have arm or leg weakness, speech trouble, or headache. Because this started suddenly and was first noticed on waking, you should be checked urgently to rule out a more serious cause. Also, if it is Bell’s palsy, treatment is most helpful when started early. Your right eye also needs prompt attention because not closing fully can let it dry out and become injured.

TRIAGE: D
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: 89%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Sudden weakness of the entire right side of your face, especially not being able to fully close the right eye, can happen with Bell’s palsy (a facial nerve problem), and the recent cold makes that possible. Bell’s palsy often affects both the forehead/eye and mouth on one side. However, a new facial droop can sometimes be caused by a stroke, even if you do not have arm or leg weakness, speech trouble, or headache. Because this started suddenly today, it should be checked urgently. Quick treatment matters both if it is a stroke and if it is Bell’s palsy, since early treatment may help recovery and your eye may need protection if it cannot close fully.

TRIAGE: D
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: 91%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): This pattern—weakness of the whole right side of the face, including trouble closing the eye, after a recent cold—sounds more like Bell’s palsy (inflammation of the facial nerve) than a stroke. Bell’s palsy often causes mouth droop and inability to fully close one eye. However, any new facial droop that started suddenly should be treated urgently because stroke cannot be ruled out from symptoms alone. The eye also needs protection if it will not close fully, because it can dry out and be injured. Because this began today, getting checked promptly is important.

TRIAGE: D
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: 82%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms sound most like a problem with the right facial nerve, often called Bell’s palsy, especially since it affects both your forehead/eye closure and mouth and happened after a recent cold. That pattern is less typical of a stroke than facial weakness with arm/leg weakness or speech trouble. Even so, new one-sided facial weakness should be assessed promptly. Bell’s palsy is often treated best if started early, and not being able to fully close your eye can let it dry out and become irritated.

TRIAGE: C
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: 86%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms sound most consistent with Bell’s palsy, which is sudden weakness of one side of the face from irritation of the facial nerve, often after a recent viral illness. The fact that you cannot fully close the right eye and the whole right side of the face is weak makes this more likely than a stroke, especially since you do not have arm or leg weakness, speech trouble, or headache. Even so, new facial weakness should be checked promptly, because treatment for Bell’s palsy works best when started early, and a clinician needs to confirm it is not something more serious. Protect the right eye from drying out or being injured until you are seen.

TRIAGE: C
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: 86%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): This pattern most suggests Bell’s palsy, a sudden weakness of the facial nerve, especially because the whole right side of your face is involved (including trouble closing the eye) and you had a recent cold. It is less typical for a stroke when the forehead/eye are affected and there are no arm/leg weakness or speech problems. Even so, new facial weakness should be checked promptly because treatment for Bell’s palsy works best when started early, ideally within 72 hours, and a clinician should confirm it is not something more serious. Protect the right eye until you’re seen, since incomplete closure can dry or injure it.

TRIAGE: C
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): This pattern—sudden weakness of the entire right side of the face, including trouble fully closing the eye, especially after a recent cold—can happen with Bell’s palsy, which is a problem with the facial nerve. But a new facial droop that started suddenly can also be caused by a stroke, and that cannot be safely ruled out from this message alone. Also, Bell’s palsy treatment works best when started early, and an eye that will not fully close can be injured if it dries out. Because this is new one-sided facial weakness, it needs urgent in-person evaluation now.

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): This pattern most often fits Bell’s palsy, which is sudden weakness of one side of the face from irritation of the facial nerve. The fact that you can’t fully close the right eye and the whole right side of the face seems involved, plus a recent cold, makes that more likely than a typical stroke pattern. Even so, it still needs prompt medical evaluation, because treatment for Bell’s palsy works best if started early (ideally within 72 hours), and the eye may need protection if it doesn’t close well.

- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms most strongly suggest Bell’s palsy, which is weakness of the facial nerve and can happen after a recent viral illness. The fact that the whole right side of your face is affected, including not being able to fully close the eye, fits that pattern more than a typical stroke. However, sudden new one-sided facial weakness can still be a stroke warning sign, and that needs urgent evaluation because timing matters. Also, if this is Bell’s palsy, treatment is most helpful when started early, and your right eye may need protection since it is not closing completely.

Bell’s palsy is a facial nerve problem that can come on suddenly and often improves, but it should be checked **promptly**, because treatment works best early and your eye may need protection. That said, any **new sudden facial droop** can sometimes be a **stroke**, and that cannot be ruled out from a message alone.
